### Jacks — 2025-05-10T02:19:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-352237>

I've been thinking a lot about transgenerational trauma and its effect on the subsequent generations' heteroplasmy. This is after reading some of the blogs and listening to some of the Q&As and podcasts recently. Also thinking about what Jack was saying about evolution speeding up with time and wondering how we can possibly get the next generation back to health and heal them from all this transgenerational trauma. The whole Covid debacle has caused additional trauma on us all.

I was also trying to figure out what happened in my parents and grandparents lives that was subsequently passed down to me and my sibs (and families). My parents both out beat their parents and grandparents in longevity - both living into their 90's. I think my father had Aspergers (my diagnosis!) which seems typical of many academics of his generation, of which he was one - just not diagnosed, but definitely awkward socially and eccentric in many ways and unable to connect emotionally. I don't know much about his parents as he never really talked about them - his mother died in a home, when I was little - maybe 4 or 5. I remember visiting her there once - she was in a wheelchair and that is all I remember - but she was one of 10 or 12 children from 2 marriages (the first mother died). She can't have been older than 75-80 I think. His father worked in the potteries in the Midlands, left school at 12, and taught himself many things. My maternal grandmother passed in her 80s of pneumonia in hospital (all I recall) while living with us. She was an only child and had a breakdown when my mother was 6 or 7, as she felt a duty to take care of her father, but was traipsing around the country due to her husbands frequently changing jobs. Of course my grandparents grew up during the first world war, and my mothers father was a survivor of The Battle of Somme. He had a brother that died in the war. My parents of course grew up during WW II, with all the rations and trauma of bombings and gas masks, my mother moving out of the south/London area to be safer. Moving along to more recent generations, one of my bros has diagnosed himself with Aspergers, and one nephew from my other brothers marriage was diagnosed with it too. Both my bro and nephew are exceptional, mathematicians, and keen chess players and puzzle solvers. So this trait appears to be passed down the male line in our family as my kids were spared (not that they don't have other health issues). I had always thought it was related to something genetic in the male line, but reading Jacks blog on autism and thinking about it, then perhaps not.

### Jacks — 2025-05-24T18:35:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-353260>

Working slowly through the Tensegrity Blogs. These are quite challenging to say the least, as lots of different physics concepts, such as solitons, which were not discussed in physics when I studied it years ago. I understand about the oscillations and waves and resonance stuff from seismology, as earthquake waves do similar things in the earth layers and similar concepts are used in medicine for imaging inside the body - but the idea of our bodies using this for signaling, I find very interesting - but then how else could the body signal to the parts not exposed to native emfs (other than use of hormones).

So yesterday I was reading one blog and it answered a question I've been mulling over for a while. Why does Jack say be outside during 9-12 am sunlight? Why not 12-3 pm as it would be the same available sunlight frequencies, surely? Well in #12 the answer lies, I believe. In particular, the construction of lipid rafts in cell membranes at this time!

Also reading over the "How does the Leptin Rx Work?" blog which I had not read before - the Leptin Reset is brilliant, to be honest and Uncle Jack should receive recognition at the highest level for putting this amazing protocol together, weaving all the disparate parts together. Using the idea of neuroplasticity (aka like Merzenich's work that I used to read about), and rewiring the brain along an ancient pathway to become leptin sensitive again, by hooking in to circadian timing (light exposure) with respect to eating times first thing in the AM. Then finishing eating enough hours before dark/sleep to trigger the correct hormone release from hypothalmus at night to release growth hormones for autophagy.

I think being out in the sun a lot these days and reading the blogs consistently (also out in the sun) is helping more pieces to fall into place.

A few more blogs in the Tensegrity series to finish, then perhaps on to Brain Gut, EMF, or Energy and Epigenetics next, but I think I will have to come back to the tensegrity series again, as many fundamental and difficult concepts are discussed. Repetition is good. I notice how Uncle Jack weaves many concepts into all the blogs so you get the built in repetition, so eventually it sinks in to all us ex-MKUltra-ed dummies.

### Jacks — 2025-05-29T02:40:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-353497>

Wow - got really itchy burning skin on my forearms while out in the sun around midday. Very strange as I've been out in the sun a lot recently (for hours at the hottest part of the day) and on much hotter days than today in my garden or out hiking. However, I was in a different location than normal. I took a friend get an X-ray at an Urgent Care Center, so there was lots of waiting around - I was in the building for about 30 mins (but covered up), but when I went outside to get some sun while waiting, within 5-10 mins of sitting outside on a bench, I started to get a burning, itchy sensation on my then exposed forearms. I suppose it could be heat rash as it was in a concrete/built up location just outside the building, so maybe hotter than my garden, or could it be related to EMFs/Wifi from the hospital building? Perhaps I'll have to take my meters with me when I go places now.

Edited: Or could it be related to eating a slice or two of grapefruit before I left! Just read the juice can cause photosensitization to the sun - so that seems the most plausible explanation, and perhaps a little juice was left on my hands and got on my arms when I pushed my sleeves up when I sat outside.

### Jacks — 2025-05-29T19:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-353530>

So got to thinking last night about grapefruit juice and what makes your skin photosensitive - supposedly to UV light. So I looked up today what compounds cause this and this is what I found: psoralens and furocoumarins. Then I started to wonder what their absorption spectra were. I have heard that they "interfere" with some medications (contraindicated).

Found this article but costly to buy

### [Activating and Fluorescent Wave-lengths of Furocoumarins : Psoralens - Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/185382a0)

BIOLOGICAL photosensitized reactions are known to result from the absorption of a wide spectrum of radiation. The energy and therefore the wave-length of light absorbed and the biological changes occurring in photosensitized reactions are obviously closely related. A fundamental law of...

 www.nature.com

But then I found this:

[https://www.researchgate.net/publication/8575396\_Photobiophysics\_of\_Furocoumarins](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/8575396_Photobiophysics_of_Furocoumarins)

Says "Furocoumarins (psoralens) are photosensitizers of plant origin, which increase the sensitivity of biological objects to near ultraviolet (UV-A, 320-400 nm). In combination with UV-A, they are successfully used for treating many dermal and autoimmune diseases (PUVA therapy and photophoresis). Along with therapeutic effects, the furocoumarin photochemotherapy induces a number of side-effects (erythema, edema, hyperpigmentation, and premature aging of skin)."

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-02T18:13:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-353750>

WHY DO LADIES BLEED?

The leptin-melanocortin pathway is a central regulator of mammalian energy balance, controlling food intake, metabolism, hormone production, fertility, and body weight. Leptin, produced by adipose tissue, signals energy availability to the hypothalamus, where it activates the melanocortin system, particularly via melanocortin-4 receptors (MC4R). This pathway, conserved across ALL mammals, evolved to ensure survival in fluctuating environments by balancing energy storage and expenditure. Epigenetic and genetic disruptions in this pathway lead to severe obesity, underscoring its role in maintaining photo-bioelectric harmony.

In the context of reproduction, the leptin-melanocortin pathway acts as an energy gatekeeper or energy capacitor, ensuring that pregnancy, a massive energy investment, only occurs when energy reserves are sufficient. Leptin levels rise with fat stores, signaling to the hypothalamus that the body can support a fetus. Just as a human is born obese to finish brain development postnatally, leptin is critical in human fertility. In female mammals, this pathway links energy status to reproductive cycles, with menstruation serving as a photo-bioelectric reset mechanism when conception does not occur. But why shed blood? Why not reabsorb the endometrium, as most mammals do in their estrous cycles?

Think about the blogs and all the things about iron and oxygen from the GOE.

The GOE predates mammalian evolution but sculpted what systems they should have. Why? The GOE introduced oxygen as a metabolic game-changer, driving the evolution of heme proteins, oxygen-binding molecules like hemoglobin and myoglobin, which enabled efficient energy production and oxygen transport.

With their iron-porphyrin cores, Heme proteins evolved to scavenge and metabolize oxygen, protecting cells from its corrosive effects while harnessing it for oxidative phosphorylation. This innovation was critical for multicellular life, particularly for mammals, whose high metabolic rates demanded robust oxygen delivery systems.

In menstruation, heme proteins in blood (e.g., hemoglobin) play a pivotal role. Menstrual blood loss involves shedding heme-rich endometrial tissue, which is laden with iron and oxygen-binding capacity. While seemingly wasteful, this process may have evolved as a photo-bioelectric adaptation to manage oxygen and iron homeostasis, leveraging the GOE’s legacy of heme protein functionality. This idea may not stimulate you to understand what menopause really is..........

Let’s explore how this ties into the evolutionary advantage of menstruation.

Menstruation as a Photo-Bioelectric Adaptation for chronic winning in a chaotic world.

Menstruation ensures that energy is allocated efficiently in female mammals, particularly in species like primates with high reproductive costs. The leptin-melanocortin pathway monitors energy availability, and in humans, where spontaneous decidualization occurs, the endometrium prepares for pregnancy each cycle regardless of conception. If energy reserves are insufficient, signaled by low leptin and melanin levels, the pathway inhibits pregnancy, triggering menstruation to reset the system. Shedding the endometrium, rather than reabsorbing it, prevents the accumulation of metabolic byproducts (e.g., ROS, lipid peroxidation products) that could disrupt mitochondrial stability and increase entropy, aligning with the thread’s emphasis on minimizing cellular chaos.

From a photo-bioelectric perspective, the endometrium’s preparation involves light-driven processes. I always comment that sunlight (UV, IRA, NIR) activates VDR and CCO, stabilizing mtDNA and reducing ROS (the first evolutionary bomb in the GOE). In the endometrium, similar light-driven mechanisms likely regulate cellular turnover, with UPEs (200–800 nm) coordinating gene expression via wave function collapse, as discussed in the thread (third bomb of GOE evolution). Menstruation clears out this tissue when pregnancy doesn’t occur, preventing the buildup of light-stressed cells that could impair future reproductive success. This reset mechanism ensures that each cycle starts with a clean slate, optimizing energy allocation for high-cost pregnancies in primates and humans.

Heme Proteins, Iron Homeostasis, and Quantum Coherence: Menstrual blood loss, rich in heme proteins like hemoglobin, serves a biophysical purpose tied to the GOE’s legacy. Heme proteins evolved to manage oxygen’s corrosive effects, and in menstruation, their shedding may protect against oxidative stress in the reproductive tract. The endometrium, a highly vascular tissue, accumulates iron and oxygen during the buildup. If reabsorbed (as in estrous cycles), this iron could fuel Fenton reactions, generating ROS that damage mtDNA and increase entropy—a risk highlighted in the thread (third bomb, IV Vitamin C fueling Fenton reactions).

By shedding this tissue, menstruation expels excess iron, reduces oxidative stress, and maintains mitochondrial stability in the uterus, a critical organ for reproductive fitness.

Quantum mechanically, heme proteins in menstrual blood may act as quantum transducers. My notes in today's X thread below teach you that collagen’s triple helix entangles photons and supports proton tunneling (seventh bomb of the GOE). Similarly, heme’s iron-porphyrin ring can entangle electrons and photons, absorbing UV light to emit UPEs that signal cellular turnover. This quantum signaling may coordinate the shedding process during menstruation, ensuring coherence in the endometrial reset. While energetically costly, the loss of heme-rich blood aligns with the thread’s emphasis on light-driven coherence, removing light-stressed, ROS-laden tissue to protect the quantum harmony of the reproductive system.

I also did not know that layer 1 of the neocortex is unmyelinated - that was a surprise! The blog lays the foundation for why humans get the diseases they get now - these complex regions are the first areas to lose energy as they were last to develop in humans (immune system and neocortex). So they go offline first.

Also started to wonder if all the demyelination diseases are related to our brains effort to try to detect the background natural EMFs (Schumann etc.) which are now all muddled with all the nnEMF signals we are being bombarded with - hence rendering our brain MORE sensitive to them - but the process backfiring as this results in disease and a negative feedback loop.

Other news - had a setback last week with one knee (ACL injured 30+ years ago - so definitely scars there). It swelled up and felt stiff and hard to bend, but no pain, so I had no idea what I had injured - but it resulted from doing some MovNat floor exercises for hips/knees/ankles - so perhaps pulled/strained a ligament somewhere. Anyway, a week later it is back to normal - grounding, sun, and cold really helped remove the swelling completely. I feel happy that I am able to not panic about such injuries, just apply the basics, and let nature do the rest - thanks Uncle Jack for instilling confidence in our self healing by following nature's rules.

My daughter is in Costa Rica and Panama this week on an adventure with her GS troop enjoying thunderstorms, hot springs, volcanoes, and various water sports. Would have loved to have accompanied them, but then again, the idea of sticking to someone else's agenda/schedule was not appealing.

### Jacks — 2025-08-03T07:14:47+0300

Just got back from a trip to old blighty - it was good to see family and for my kids to reconnect with their cousins after 5-6 years of not traveling across the pond. However, was shocked by the level of surveillance - I thought here was bad, but there it is apalling. Luckily the weather was fantastic the first week so got lots of sunshine and staying in a quiet spot in the countryside with a big garden helped with jetlag. Visited my hometown again up north, which included more clearing out of my mothers property to get ready for sale. The north is definitely better than the south, though the university there is now overrun by foreign students apparently (mostly Asians paying high tuition fees). Going back again alone for a family wedding in a few weeks time, which may end up being my very last time there, given the state of the world these days. Spending the intervening weeks improving redox as much as possible. Air travel takes its toll, so you have to be deliberate in your choices and do what you need to do, to prepare and protect yourself.

On another note, just been noticing lots of dogs in the neighborhood having hind leg issues and losing their ability to stand and walk - I think they are suffering from demyelination.

Just finished reading one of the latest blogs: DM #63, which is really relevant to me. Have been focusing on trying to heal my eyes - I have floaters and presbyopia. I seem to have improved my color vision this past year, but the floaters are still there - perhaps slightly better, definitely not worse, which I suppose is something, given living in Chernobyl. However, I was curious about Uncle Jack discussing my haplotype (H) and saying CT was less effective for this haplotype. I was under the impression that H was uncoupled and so CT was very useful for healing. Seems may be not - at least for eyes. However, this does explain the difficulty I have had in trying to improve my eyes as Jack says H haplotypes have reduced melanin and lower oxidative stress resilience in the eye and have increased ROS accumulation therefore higher risk for AMD and cataracts. I was wondering if eye color has any effect on this? The rest of my family, other than my mother have blue/green/hazel eyes. My mother and I (and both my offspring) have dark brown eyes. Does this mean we may have more melanin in our eyes? My mother only started to get cataracts in her 90's but ended up not doing any surgery in the end.

### Jacks — 2025-10-25T23:08:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-361609>

It has been a few weeks since I last posted. The MovNat workshop was really fun at times but some of it was held in a poorly lit gym environment and there was less outside time than I hoped for, so is not something I would recommend or do again at least at this location. In fact, I hurt my knee (again) on the last day after the 2-hour warm up in the poorly lit gym. Ugh! I guess not surprising. The knee swelled up really badly so I could not bend it - no pain just tight. Ice, rest, and sun helped and I was happy I did not sign up for the certification. However, finally was able to do the cross-squat get up at the event, but unable to do the foot pinch, so wouldn't have been able to complete all the cert tests. I was camping out of the city in a location that had the lowest emf I've been in for a long time - fantastic - I really liked that part of Josephine County down by the cold Rogue River. My knee is finally feeling good again this week, with all the swelling gone - have been resting, icing, sunning, grounding, and building up my walking capability. I started to wonder if years ago, when I supposedly tore my ACL, that if I had skipped seeing a doctor, it would have just calmed down on its own (like what just happened) and I wouldn't have undergone surgery. It is that Pandora's Box situation - you go to a doctor, get a diagnosis, then are drawn into their proposed solution, which usually involves an expensive treatment - but you go along with it as you believe them and don't know any better. Sigh! That was about 35 years ago and I was naive.

Have been reading Nick Lane's Power, Sex & Suicide book. Also trying to finish up the Time blog series. Finished #23 yesterday - excellent - all about the 4 levels of protein structure - and how the protein structure affects how the protein interacts with the earth's magnetic field and the sun's input. How the protein's are structured affects the polarization and dipole moments of them, and thus the interaction with the native fields (electromagnetic) from earth and sun. I did not know that the sun's spectrum has changed with time - less UV on earth in earlier times (makes sense) when bacteria and archea's had their heyday. So much packed into this one blog.

Off on a week-long camping trip soon. Looking forward to camping and being outdoors, though not the long drive. Should be sunny during the days, but cold at night, and I will get better UV light due to elevation. Just picked up the book The Universal One - think I'll take it along for the ride.

Also, have been following the Duke Report substack for a while. He has a nice website that lists hundreds of books he has read and reviewed here at [https://dukereportbooks.com/](https://dukereportbooks.com/) which include his synopsis of the content. He has just started doing short explainers (using AI notebook I think) for some of the books in a podcast format so you get the gist of lots of controversial books really quickly. His aim is to educate and get information out as fast as possible to wake the world up. I am finding it a useful resource.

### Jacks — 2025-12-09T20:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-363734>

Checking in for first time in a while. Counting the days till the solstice now - less than 2 weeks. Was hoping to get away with DH to warmer climes due to his asthma issues, but that may have to wait till after the holidays now due to changes in peoples plans. But it is cold, so I'm enjoying that right now. However, we've been having very poor air quality which makes outdoor activities less appealing. My 2nd BIL (out of 3) to get gall bladder surgery did so about a week ago - apparently it was an emergency and there was gangrene involved. I guess that is not surprising being jabbed up to the bajesus belt....

Went camping near Canyonlands, Utah last month. Last time I was there was over 10 years ago. I enjoyed the sunlight (move UV as higher elevation) and fantastic scenery (of course) and seeing old friends, but boy is it dry out there - I'd forgotten how much so. Saw very little wildlife - a few bunnies, ravens, bluejay, a fox, and some deer. Was thinking about the electric universe model and how all that material from the Colorado Plateau is gone - "the erosion" is astounding and hard to fathom being caused by the dinky Colorado and Green Rivers cutting down - more like an electric scar. I wore shorts and t-shirts, but my friends (from Denver) covered up and smeared on sunscreen & wore sunglasses and hats - being ever so fearful of the sun. I explained about how good it is for us - they are too afraid of melanoma recurrence for one of them, which I informed them was due to fake light. They were still convinced it was from childhood sun burns. Oh well... They told me a mutual aquaintance living in Flagstaff (we knew from UU) was recently diagnosed with Parkinsons. Don't think Flagstaff is a good place to go. I was able to keep up with them while hiking no problem, using diaphragmatic breathing through nose up the steep climbs, despite me coming from sea-level and having a recent knee injury. They were huffing and puffing with mouths open and supposedly they exercise a lot. I heard about all their health problems and those of their friends in Denver. Lots of bad stuff happening all around it seems.

I am almost done reading the Reality blogs. The one about athlete injuries was good - reminded me of why I hurt my knee last month in the gym at the MovNat event. I just did not realize how quickly a soft tissue injury can happen under poor lighting. Reality #11 explains SHG - which again relates back to that tertiary and quaternary protein structure and how the body can generate UV biophotons.

Realized I have not tackled listening to all the old webinars so I am starting on those now. Probably should have done that in conjunction with the blogs I was reading them, though I did jump around a bit with the blog series. I think the Reality blogs may be the last ones I need to read of the old free series. Perhaps there are some CPC blogs still to read .... After that I will start the Patreon/Optimal Klub blogs. Also reading The Universal One and Alan Turing: The Enigma. And trying to get out as much as I can during these low sunlight times.

### Jacks — 2026-05-05T00:26:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-373052>

I have not posted in a while. I've been busy reading old & new blogs, listening to webinars & Q&A's, trying to keep up on the forum with all the new information on the SAA & defragging from deuterium, and trying to assimilate all the information and make a plan.

We were going to check out various areas in Oregon this summer for a potential place to move to temporarily, but now considering Washington as well as that may be much more favorable for the magnetic field. The Pacific Northwest volcanoes from Northern CA north are stratovolcanoes that produce mostly andesitic and dacitic lava flows (composite cones) these are more silicic and have less ferromagnesian minerals than basalts (therefore lower magnetic susceptibility). Shield volcanoes produce the most basaltic lavas (highest magnetic susceptibility) and these occur at Mid Ocean Ridges, Large Igneous Provinces (such as the Columbia River basalts, Deccan traps etc.), or shield volcanoes such as at hotspots (Hawaii, Iceland, Canary Islands). The Columbia River basalts are Miocene in age - pretty old 6-17 million years ago. The Miocene (23-5.3 Ma) experienced several geomagnetic reversals, but was similar to todays at the start of the Miocene. Will keep on working this research. May be ophiolitic basaltic rocks nearer the coast but not sure.

Listening to the April 2026 webinar, Jack mentions that living near a volcano or on magnetized volcanic rocks is more important now than worrying about distance from airports. Wondering if this also applies to military bases, as Washington has a lot of them in the Tacoma and Seattle corridor and northwards. Currently active volcanoes are inland of this valley, however, the Columbia River Basalts are mostly to the south near the CR gorge area. Need to check out more geologic maps and go measure magnetic fields with a compass on our road trip.

### Jacks — 2026-05-05T02:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-373066>

> Klamath sucks in the winter, lots of snow. If you live out of town expect to get stuck.
> BUT in S Oregon . . . Medford is close in sunny days, no military base, on the I5 for easy north/south travel.
> We also have a volcano . . . AND an In and Out . . .

Mount Mazama (Crater Lake) blew its top about 7,700 years ago to form Crater Lake and last erupted 4,800 years ago (rhyodacite dome in the Lake). Visited once, years ago - it is a stratovolcano so lower magnetic susceptibility compared to shield volcanoes. However, I remember visiting some lava tubes in northeastern CA about 10 years ago with basaltic lava tubes forming caves that you can walk through (pahoehoe & aa flows), NE of the Medicine Lake volcanic center which is a shield volcano. The lava tubes are apparently around 30-40,000 years old but there are some amazingly fresh looking cinder cones. Unfortunately it is a desert in that neck of the woods (on the edge of the Great Basin) so not so attractive.

I have friends in Bend, and visited in the last few years. It is high desert there. You are near the Newberry Volcanic area which is a broad shield shaped composite volcano - been erupting for 600,000 years with latest only 1,300 years ago - but that was a rhyolite flow cooled to obsidian (which are really cool to see - but very silicic, so low ferromagnesian mineral content). You are right - there are basaltic and basaltic andesites there too, but this volcanic area has the whole gamut of volcanic rock types - hence composite. Never been but there is an Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS) power plant there, kind of like at the Geysers north of where I am.

Need to check out more geologic maps and go measure magnetic fields with a compass on our road trip.

WHY IS THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST STILL ON MY LIST?

The Juan de Fuca plate is a small tectonic plate located off the coast of Washington and Oregon in the Pacific Northwest region of North America. It's an oceanic plate that's being subducted (pushed) beneath the North American continental plate now. This process can lead to significant earthquakes, as seen in the Cascadia Subduction Zone, which runs along the Pacific Northwest coast. The plate's movement and fragmentation, can impact earthquake risk and volcanic behavior in the region.

Key Factors Contributing to Fragmentation: Intense Pressure and Heat: As the plate sinks into the mantle, it experiences increasing pressure and heat, weakening the plate and leading to cracks and splits.

Subduction Process: The plate's descent into the mantle creates stress and deformation, causing it to break apart into smaller fragments. Plate Interaction: The interaction between the Juan de Fuca Plate and the North American Plate leads to the formation of large fractures and faults, further contributing to the plate's fragmentation .

Implications of Plate Fragmentation: Earthquake & volcanic risk rise
Risk: for catastrophe is low.

Geological Changes: Probable. I'd expect creation of new fault boundaries and redistributing stress across the subduction zone.

Tectonic Evolution: The fragmentation provides insight into how Earth's crust evolves and how subduction zones develop over time and how they link to local volcanism.

### Jacks — 2026-05-05T19:39:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-373145>

Another thing about the Juan De Fuca plate is that younger and younger oceanic crust (therefore hotter & more buoyant) is being subducted as the JDF plate gets smaller and the spreading ridge on its west side gets closer to the coast, which might add additional stress to the down-going slab. The recurrence period for a great earthquake on the Casacadia Subduction Zone is about 500 years, so we are due up for another big one in the next 100-150 years. I think the probability of a complete rupture (~M 9) is about 10-20% in the next 50 years. Earthquake size is related to area of fault rupture so fault length is a good proxy. This is why subduction zones produced the largest earthquakes (and tsunamis), as the rupture area tends to be much greater than for normal or strike-slip faults due to the low angle on the fault interface (between crust and down-going oceanic plate). Ductile behavior of rock in the crust occurs at 15-30 km depth (it varies with heat flow) which limits the extent of brittle deformation.

### Jacks — 2026-05-05T19:53:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-373147>

Update on wildlife my location. Seeing Monarch butterflies recently in my garden - which is a little out of season, but I think they must follow the magnetic field lines during their migration. Could be stragglers coming from further south I suppose, rather than lost! Also see a few other varieties of butterflies such as skippers, cabbage whites, and some CA sisters.

We have a lot of birds in our garden still, including humming birds - and now house finches this year for the first time and boy do they have a sweet-sounding song. Found a dead baby bird a week or two ago that must have fallen from a nest. Lots of crows in our 'hood, not surprising, since they are chock full of melanin and seem to thrive. Saw two owls on a morning walk in a tall tree nearby. Been getting my aged bunny (9.5 years old) out in the garden most days and he seems to be doing well - still pretty black and molted a bit recently, though has slight cataracts in his eyes from previous indoor living.

Plants are doing pretty well this year as we have had plentiful rain (about 120% normal) and fruit trees/bushes are producing well for the most part. I had a lot of miner bees in our backyard earlier this year and now the big black bees and honey bees are out as well as smaller native bees, but mostly in the front yard where I grow bee- and bird-friendly flowers. Our 30+ ft tall century plant, which flowered last year, is still standing and dropping seeds now.

### Jacks — 2026-05-05T20:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-optimal-journal.25708/post-373152>

Have been developing a really nice tan, but suspect only melanating on the outside. DH has been getting his chest out too and is well-melanated now. His breathing has improved. Doing some interval runs and has increased his expiration capacity. We have been getting some good UV days, but now changed to cloudy cool days - hoping the summer weather pattern isn't developing yet as that limits the amount of good sun without having to migrate east or north. Can't wait to try to volcano/beach protocols in ES - I think that is what we both need (like yesterday). We are both ready for a change and itching for it.

DDW: Started DDW a week ago, but noticed slight light-headedness (no pun intended) on occasion after drinking it on the first 5 days. Had to sit down and held my dog close and it passed quickly - noted that JK covered this towards end of April 2026 Q&A with someone else having issues after starting to drink it - so perhaps a blockage of D in CSF somewhere. Currently diluting 50 ppm with spring water purportedly 139 ppm from a Mt. Shasta spring.

Shake, Rattle & Roll: Started chewing mastic, but that caused an issue with a tooth I had problems with a few years ago, but I only had 2 nights of pain and it is now back to normal. Was also ruminating over a personal issue that I had to let go of - which took awhile. Also added ice-crunching, skipping, singing bowl, TOOL, yawning, humming, upper body movements (pushing and pulling) etc.

Results:

-   Bristols hovering between 3-5 these days with some 4's.
-   Dark morning urine.
-   Have a slightly puffy eye on left side in the morning but fingers not stiff. My eyes are what I am focusing on with morning light (increasing morning hours outside for photorepair before 9 am) and ice-water face dunks.
-   Brain is working better and better - cognition of reading improves when outside.
-   Dreaming most nights.
-   Temperature Regulation Good. Mostly outside in shorts and t-shirt throughout the year morning, day, and evening, so adapted to climate here. Have cool skin on outside but feel warm inside.
-   Teeth appear to be whitening.

Don't measure HRV but maybe should as have had some heart arrhythmias in the past.
Adding more MCT oil to increase beta oxidation pathway.

Reading: Eating the Sun, Letting go, & finshing up the Hypoxia blog series. Half way through the DM series as started from beginning again. BTC & QE series up next. For fun, reading The Alternative Asimov's - some of the ideas in his novelettes are quantum physics based which sometimes resonate with something I just read in the blogs! Up next is The Earth and I, maybe another Becker book, or perhaps Cunningham's new book Inversion. I think the ECDO and gyroscopic flip of the earth is more likely than just crustal slippage - that makes no sense with subduction plates going down up to 660 km into the mantle in some areas (and possibly deeper) as that pins the crust & mantle together, I would think.

# Thread: Do you use sound to mitohack nature?  I do.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-use-sound-to-mitohack-nature-i-do.25709/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-12T01:45:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-use-sound-to-mitohack-nature-i-do.25709/post-297144>

Nature is a natural antidepressant. Scientists found that an overwhelming amount of evidence points to dramatic improvements in both mental and physical health when a person is exposed to the sounds of nature. [https://www.pnas.org/content/118/14/e2013097118](https://www.pnas.org/content/118/14/e2013097118)

Every time I paint I use sounds of nature to help. Every time I do a new TV show, as I did today, I use the sounds of Nature in the background. [https://www.pnas.org/content/118/14/e2013097118](https://www.pnas.org/content/118/14/e2013097118)

### caroline — 2021-04-12T04:43:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-use-sound-to-mitohack-nature-i-do.25709/post-297153>

Every morning at sunrise we walk on our seawall. It goes out into the ocean for approx 2km so we have the surf and waves crashing on both sides of us as we walk towards the rising sun.

For us - this is nature at its finest .......all the sites and sounds. The ocean, the beaches, the birds and the surfers. Yesterday there was three kangaroos at sunrise feeding at the beach. Kangaroos often hop down the main street of our village at sunrise!

The thing is ...every single day is different - and we even love it when it is raining.

There is natures's beauty in every season and we are thriving.

Amidst all this wonder and beauty and the sounds of nature ....we constantly see people talking on their iPhone as they walk.

They are completely oblivious to all this joy .....and I reckon they deserve whatever they get......

BTW ....why is everyone in Lycra - head to foot????? and of course everyone is wearing sunglasses. UGH

### Freebird — 2021-04-13T04:32:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-use-sound-to-mitohack-nature-i-do.25709/post-297208>

Awesome scene.

And: I would be happy if cell phones were outlawed.

### John Schumacher — 2021-04-13T18:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-use-sound-to-mitohack-nature-i-do.25709/post-297225>

Since vacations, even a drive away, have been difficult for me financial - do more to the need to keep things going, paying for school and life with at least a couple of jobs. The time away from being able to earn a living was the most expensive.
So forty five years ago, I purchased a record player from the thrift store, then over time I purchased LP records of environmental sounds.
Since, I've had insomnia for thirty years (from puberty to my mid forties), sleeping fours hours or so every three days, I had a lot of "free-time" for prayer & meditation. I would play these environmental sound tracks and imagine what it might be like.

# Thread: Are sunglasses necessary when kayaking or sailing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-sunglasses-necessary-when-kayaking-or-sailing.25710/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sindre — 2021-04-12T19:41:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-sunglasses-necessary-when-kayaking-or-sailing.25710/post-297175>

Hi.

Are sunglasses necessary when kayaking or sailing? Sunglasses only came into being in the start of the 1900s, so I presume our ancestors didn't go blind while sailing long voyages. I am planning on going kayaking this summer in the Oslo fjord here in Norway. I am planning on wearing a cap most of the time, but there is reflection from the water. Are sunglasses necessary?

Thanks in advance.

### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-12T21:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-sunglasses-necessary-when-kayaking-or-sailing.25710/post-297186>

If you have a lot of solar time they may play a role......I would not wear them all the time.

### Johan Lindøm — 2021-09-20T22:35:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-sunglasses-necessary-when-kayaking-or-sailing.25710/post-304003>

Hei Sindre! Går det bra. Sunglasses are not required. Especially not in August in Norway! If you get tired in your eyes, you can consider glasses. In that case, I wear my blue light blocking glasses - the brightest. As far as I understand it is too much blue light from light spectra that creates the ALBEDO effect and that can make us snow blind.

At least I experience that my blue light-blocked glasses work and relieve any stress I feel on my eyes when I experience strong sun and reflection of light from snow or sea. Since I have become accustomed to getting sunlight in my eyes, I need less protection. The more sun I get on my body and in my eyes, the better I experience withstanding strong light. This summer I have hardly worn glasses outside, or I only wear my regular glasses and only far out on my nose so I can see better.

# Thread: IF COVID WAS THE COMPLIANCE TEST WHAT IS THE NEXT MOVE?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-covid-was-the-compliance-test-what-is-the-next-move.25712/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-12T20:08:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-covid-was-the-compliance-test-what-is-the-next-move.25712/post-297178>

[https://unlimitedhangout.com/2021/0...emic-collapse-of-the-global-financial-system/](https://unlimitedhangout.com/2021/04/investigative-reports/wef-warns-of-cyber-attack-leading-to-systemic-collapse-of-the-global-financial-system/)

### John Schumacher — 2021-04-12T20:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-covid-was-the-compliance-test-what-is-the-next-move.25712/post-297180>

Dr. [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) -> Thank you for preparing a way.

### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-12T21:17:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-covid-was-the-compliance-test-what-is-the-next-move.25712/post-297184>

The WEF ran simulations around a "potential" global pandemic caused by a coronavirus. Months later it happened in 2020. Their latest simulations focus on a global financial cyber attack. Last night on 60mins, Jerome Powell alluded to this. It's as if they're coordinating in plain sight.

This makes perfect sense when you understand who got McKinley elected, how McKinley got taken out, and who lost the most at that moment in Washington DC. [https://www.politico.com/story/2018/12/03/this-day-in-politics-december-3-1027800](https://www.politico.com/story/2018/12/03/this-day-in-politics-december-3-1027800)

The three men who pooled their money together to elect McKinley over Bryant in 1900 were Rockefeller, Carnegie, and JP Morgan. All Titans of industry who held monopoly power. This power was massive until...........McKinley was assassinated.

The Fed came to fruition 18 months after Standard Oil Trust was broken up.
The Supreme Court ruled against Standard “on the ground that it is a combination in unreasonable restraint of inter-State commerce,” The New York Times [explained,](https://learning.blogs.nytimes.com/on-this-day/may-15) adding that the court “thus definitely reads the word ‘unreasonable’ into the law.” The ruling, that only “unreasonable” restraint of trade constitutes a monopoly, was received by antitrust advocates as a narrow decision that favored the trusts. The Times reported that “the opinion prevailed that the decision was distinctly favorable to ‘big business.’”

The court’s decision forced Standard to break into 34 independent companies spread across the country and abroad. Many of these companies have since split, folded, or merged; today, the primary descendants of Standard include ExxonMobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips.

-   Despite early attempts in the U.S. after the revolutionary war to form a central bank by Alexander Hamilton, these efforts failed due to political unfeasibility.
-   The banking panic of 1907, and at the urging of **J.P. Morgan** and other **prominent financiers like Rockefeller and Carnegie**, Congress eventually formed the Federal Reserve Act in 1913, establishing the Fed as America's central bank.
-   Recall that Rockefeller after his testimony said that he would see to it that the government would face retribution for this decision. The Supreme Court Ruled against Rockefeller eventually in 1909. Standard Oil of New Jersey was broken up and Big Pharma formed from parts of the Stand Oil trust and remains strong in NJ ever since the break-up. My Sept 2018 webinar covers this entire situation.
-   [https://energyhistory.yale.edu/libr...ckefeller-president-standard-oil-company-1899](https://energyhistory.yale.edu/library-item/testimony-john-d-rockefeller-president-standard-oil-company-1899)

In Nov 2020, during COVID the World Economic Forum - Carnegie letter was written. See below.
[https://unlimitedhangout.com/2021/0...emic-collapse-of-the-global-financial-system/](https://unlimitedhangout.com/2021/04/investigative-reports/wef-warns-of-cyber-attack-leading-to-systemic-collapse-of-the-global-financial-system/)

[https://learning.blogs.nytimes.com/...me-court-orders-standard-oil-to-be-broken-up/](https://learning.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/05/15/may-15-1911-supreme-court-orders-standard-oil-to-be-broken-up/)

From December 1912 to December 1913, the Glass-Willis proposal was hotly debated, molded, and reshaped. By December 23, 1913, when President Woodrow Wilson signed the Federal Reserve Act into law, it stood as a classic example of compromise—"**a decentralized central bank"** that balanced the competing interests of private banks and populist sentiment. As we know the Fed is a centralized bank. But note what is recorded in history in this link:
[https://www.federalreserveeducation.org/about-the-fed/history](https://www.federalreserveeducation.org/about-the-fed/history)

Before the new central bank could begin operations, the Reserve Bank Operating Committee, comprised of Treasury Secretary William McAdoo, Secretary of Agriculture David Houston, and Comptroller of the Currency John Skelton Williams, had the arduous task of building a working institution around the bare bones of the new law. But, by November 16, 1914, the 12 cities chosen as sites for regional Reserve Banks were open for business, just as hostilities in Europe erupted into World War I.

When World War I broke out in mid-1914, U.S. banks continued to operate normally, thanks to the emergency currency issued under the Aldrich-Vreeland Act of 1908. But the greater impact in the United States came from the Reserve Banks’ ability to discount bankers acceptances. Through this mechanism, the United States aided the flow of trade goods to Europe, indirectly helping to finance the war until 1917, when the United States officially declared war on Germany, and financing our own war effort became paramount.

Following World War I, Benjamin Strong, head of the New York Fed from 1914 to his death in 1928, **_recognized that gold no longer served as the central factor in controlling credit._** Strong’s aggressive action to stem a recession in 1923 through a large purchase of government securities gave clear evidence of the power of open market operations to influence the availability of credit in the banking system. During the 1920s, the Fed began using open market operations as a monetary policy tool. During his tenure, Strong also elevated the stature of the Fed by promoting relations with other central banks, especially the Bank of England.

This tells you what the Fed really thinks about gold.

### Johan Lindøm — 2021-04-13T14:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-covid-was-the-compliance-test-what-is-the-next-move.25712/post-297214>

... they'll be blaming a cyber attack, while the story will really be about debt consolidation and hyperinflation ....global coordination and programmable digital money... that means the censorship will continue, but in a new form, this time controlled by central banks and technology companies. With the SARS virus, there have been public health authorities and technology companies that have censored, especially the truth... constantly directed by WEF. What a shit show .... thank goodness for BTC.

# Thread: HELLO FROM NEW MEMBER
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-member.25713/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Freebird — 2021-04-12T20:53:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-member.25713/post-297183>

Just getting started on the forum...

A few thoughts at the moment: Ready to move. The desert feels like a parasite. But it's better than a shitty city. The sky is nicer, even when they're chem trailing... way less cars and cell towers. I like places with crappy cell service, green plants and water ways... and sea food swimming nearby. Always loved sea food. And always loved nature! Never understood why some people don't appreciate nature.

When I was a kid, I would go around the neighborhood knocking on people's doors trying to get people together to play baseball, and all the kids would be wanting to stay inside and watch TV, on a beautiful sunny day! Weird.

I don't feel great in the desert though. Bone dry and dusty! You get extra chem trails coming up out of the powder dirt and floating around in the air. And no in-between: a few weeks of spring, if you're lucky, then: blazing hot! I like moisture in the air.

I would love to roam around with some like-minded people for a short while and see more of this beautiful country while we still can. Then possibly get the hell out -- go somewhere with a simpler, more natural and healthier environment and life style.

I've been eating some sea food (want more; I miss it), grass fed beef and fat, eggs, butter -- mostly all raw.

I've been waking up around 3:30 or 4 AM (not on purpose). Go to bed between 9:30 and 10 PM. Would like to sleep a little more each night!

Can't wait to get my feet in the grass and in the water again.

That’s it for now. Onward and upward.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/having-kids.25716/post-297204>

Hi everyone,

I don’t know if this post is appropriate for this forum but a lot of you and Jack have amazing point of views on life and all its conplexities.

I’m thinking about having kids. I know that most people will tell me it’s a personal decision but I’d like to know if anybody has interesting thoughts on the subject. At the risk of sounding really egotistic, my main concern about having kids are freedom, finance and the current state of the world we live in... which every day seems crazier and crazier...

Thanks for your help

Mike

### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-13T04:25:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/having-kids.25716/post-297207>

You lose all of them but people still choose to do it. Nature's evolution drives are quite amazing.

### axel brandt — 2021-04-16T23:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/having-kids.25716/post-297344>

Mike, I had the same questions, and tried very hard not to have any, specially because I dislike the kind of world we are living on..............but at age 45, had my one and only child, and having lost nearly all my freedom, I can tell you, it´s amazing to have them. We are wired to have them.

### Mike117 — 2021-04-23T14:01:41+0300

### John Schumacher — 2021-04-15T17:29:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-get-chem-trails-out-of-the-body.25718/post-297296>

Thank you [@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/) - detox is a very taxing biological process. Preparing the body for any detox protocol is vital to success.
On this forum, there are many who may chime in with their favorite protocol for example: [@Dan2](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20918/) -> [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/depression-and-nnemf.21987/#post-293328](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/depression-and-nnemf.21987/#post-293328)

However, the question perhaps to start with is what [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) mentions -> that is, if you don't test your biological levels, you may just pick up a gun and start shooting, but not know there was a target and you may have forgotten to aim. [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/measing-of-heteroplasmy.19726/#post-280988](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/measing-of-heteroplasmy.19726/#post-280988)

Choosing a mitochondrial health professional to help guide you is always the best; but they deserve to earn a living too. When we pay a health provider, we are offering our respect.

So if you are looking for **free** -> _Fast._

-   You can start with one meal a day at noon. This is known as intermittent fasting. I know many individuals who are doing well into their mid 80s living this life-style well.

-   Typically, a minimum of a three day water fast can be done while holding a job. It takes the body a bit of encouragement to believe -> it is not starving. After three days _(typically)_, you will not feel "hungry".

-   When you go past three days into a week or month fast, you must physiologically rest. Go on a fasting retreat where all you do is recover, go for walks in nature, breath deeply fresh clean air, drink pure mountain spring water, meditate & pray. On these types of fasts, it is very important that you follow [@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/) advice -> redox before detox. Then when you finish these types of fasts -> redox.

Go outside again, for as much as the 4-7pm time period as I can. In other words, I've decided the sun, especially the early morning sun, trumps it all.

### Sue-UK — 2021-04-16T09:58:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-get-chem-trails-out-of-the-body.25718/post-297319>

> [Freebird said:](/goto/post?id=297312)
>
> Thanks John. I've tried two meals/day for short periods of time -- on a few different occasions, but didn't do well on it at all. I have sensed that once I get to a better environment, I'll be able to flow with TMAD or even OMAD much more naturally.Then, yes, redox will be better so many other things will get in sync fairly easily.

Blood levels of contaminants like aluminium are usually higher after an overnight fast, because stored levels are being released from tissues such as fat cells and bone. For some people with high tissue and bone levels, I think OMAD leaves a long timeframe for stored levels to be being released - and if its not cleared out quickly through sweat or urine, I am concerned it could go back into storage in a worse place, such as the brain. Or that the contaminants detoxed from the brain could just go back there. The link between AD and contaminants such as aluminium is also a potential reason not to do OMAD, but its something I'm still trying to work out .... What concerns me is that when insulin has finished doing its job, it is degraded by insulin degrading enzyme, (IDE). When its not degrading insulin, IDE is clearing amyloid. Good reason not to have chronically high insulin levels, because it rarely gets the chance to clear amyloid, but my interest is whether very low levels of insulin from keto/OMAD or whatever would downregulate IDE production, and in turn the clearance
of amyloid. Might not be a problem in a place with low exposure, but in a place where contaminants like aluminium are regularly sprayed into the atmosphere, or from high levels from traffic (easy access to the brain through the olfactory bulb), that could also make a difference as to whether OMAD is a good idea.

My best advice about location is that you have to go to figure out what works. We ended up in one location before that had lots of positives, but it was only through time and experience that we learned what the negatives were. Looking back on it, I realize that some of the things that seemed negative at the time, probably were less of a big deal than I was making them out to be. As we are preparing to move back this time, we'd like to think we could just look at Zillow and pick something, or pick an area that someone else has found positive, but we know it will take going and trying and adjusting. We are considering a camper van/RV or something similar or an airbnb or shorter term rental to facilitate being nimble. Hope this helps.

I did the Borax thing once... my eye twitched for days...  I just use it in the laundry now - also, you can take 1 cup of borax and one cup of water, make a spray - spray any boards with termites and it is supposed to kill them...

For all you geeks out there - like probably John

[https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22068843/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22068843/)
**First-principles study of electronic and magnetic properties of transition metal adsorbed h-BNC2 sheets**
[Pooja Srivastava](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Srivastava+P&cauthor_id=22068843) [1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22068843/#affiliation-1) , [Mrinalini Deshpande](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Deshpande+M&cauthor_id=22068843), [Prasenjit Sen](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Sen+P&cauthor_id=22068843)
Affiliations

-   PMID: **22068843**
-   DOI: [10.1039/c1cp21974j](https://doi.org/10.1039/c1cp21974j)

**Abstract**
Adsorption of Fe, Co and Ni atoms on a hybrid hexagonal sheet of graphene and boron nitride is studied using density functional methods. Most favorable adsorption sites for these adatoms are identified for different widths of the graphene and boron nitride regions. Electronic structure and magnetic properties of the TM-adsorbed sheets are then studied in detail. The TM atoms change the electronic structure of the sheet significantly, and the resulting system can be a magnetic semiconductor, semi-metal, or a non-magnetic semiconductor depending on the TM chosen. This gives tunability of properties which can be useful in novel electronics applications. Finally, barriers for diffusion of the adatoms on the sheet are calculated, and their tendency to agglomerate on the sheet is estimated.

It seems like boron is like magnetic glue...  Way over my head though

### Penny — 2021-04-25T17:32:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-get-chem-trails-out-of-the-body.25718/post-297744>

Actually, this was even more fun... boron dopes your mito semiconductors...

[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S092151071000406X](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S092151071000406X)

**Insights into the local electronic structure of semiconducting boron carbides in the vicinity of transition metal dopants**
Author links open overlay panel[GuangfuLuoabJingLubJingLiucWai-NingMeia
P.A.Dowbenc](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S092151071000406X#!)
[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2010.06.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2010.06.001)[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S092151071000406X&orderBeanReset=true)
**Abstract**
Based on the experimentally determined pairwise site locations of transition metal atoms in doped semiconducting boron carbides, model spin-polarized electronic structure calculations indicate that some transition metal doped semiconducting boron carbides may act as excellent spin filters when used as the dielectric barrier in a magnetic tunnel junction structure. The local spin configuration of all the 3d transition metals, Ti to Cu, doped boron carbides are compared using first-principle total energy calculations on cluster and infinite icosahedral chain models. In the case of Ti and Fe doping, there may be considerable enhancements in the magneto-resistance of the heterostructure. The models also confirm that the favorable sites 3d transition metal atoms occupied are within the icosahedral cage, in a pairwise fashion on adjacent icosahedra.

### Jack Kruse — 2021-04-13T06:57:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-makes-up-150-ppm-of-blood-why-we-can-taste-it.25719/post-297211>

People can taste deuterium. This ability is linked to a physical phenomena. This physical effect is attributed to zero-point energy has been experimentally verified, such as [spontaneous emission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_emission), [Casimir force](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casimir_force), [Lamb shift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamb_shift), [magnetic moment of the electron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron_magnetic_moment) and [Delbrück scattering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delbr%C3%BCck_scattering). These effects are usually called "radiative corrections". In more complex nonlinear theories (e.g. QCD) zero-point energy can give rise to a variety of complex phenomena such as [multiple stable states](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bistability), [symmetry breaking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_symmetry_breaking), [chaos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaos_theory) and [emergence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emergence).

Deuterium laced water tastes sweeter than DDW. People with mitochondrial diseases lose this ability to taste. I have found their sense of smell and hearing are also affected. There are some interesting changes in the ear because of how much ATP hearing requires. The higher your redox is, the more discriminating your taste becomes. We test for that at KLC Destin. It is a quantum biomarker for mitochondrial heteroplasmy rate.

There is anecdotal evidence from the 1930s that the taste of pure D2O is distinct from the neutral one of pure H2O, being described mostly as 'sweet'.
Urey and Failla addressed this scientific question in 1935 [concluding authoritatively](https://science.sciencemag.org/content/81/2098/273.2) in the literature that upon tasting 'neither of them could detect the slightest difference between the taste of ordinary distilled water and the taste of pure heavy water'. What Urey and Failla did not know is that being exposed to radiation of any type increased their heteroplasmy rate and destroyed their redox potential. Mitochondria and redox potential of cells were not even discovered in 1935.

**Harold Urey** was a [physical chemist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physical_chemist) whose pioneering work on [isotopes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotope) earned him the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry) in 1934 for the discovery of [deuterium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deuterium). In 1931, he began work with the separation of isotopes that resulted in the discovery of deuterium. He worked around radiation almost his entire adult life.

During World War II, Urey turned his knowledge of isotope separation to the problem of [uranium enrichment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uranium_enrichment). He headed the group located at Columbia University that developed isotope separation using [gaseous diffusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaseous_diffusion). The method was successfully developed, becoming the sole method used in the early post-war period. After the war, Urey became a professor of chemistry at the [Institute for Nuclear Studies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Institute_for_Nuclear_Studies), and later Ryerson professor of chemistry at the [University of Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Chicago).

He did not continue his pre-war research with isotopes. He began to distrust the industrial-military complex and how they wanted to control nuclear energy in the US. After the war, he began to apply his knowledge gained with hydrogen isotopes before the war to molecular oxygen. He realized that the [fractionation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractionation) between [carbonate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbonate) and water for [oxygen-18](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxygen-18) and [oxygen-16](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxygen-16) would decrease by a factor of 1.04 between 0 and 25 C.
The ratio of the isotopes could then be used to determine average temperatures, assuming that the measurement equipment was sufficiently sensitive.

Later in life, Urey helped develop the field of [cosmochemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmochemistry) and is credited with coining the term. His work on oxygen-18 led him to develop theories about the [abundance of the chemical elements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abundance_of_the_chemical_elements) on earth, and of their abundance and evolution in the stars. Urey summarized his work in _The Planets: Their Origin and Development_ (1952). Urey speculated that the early terrestrial [atmosphere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere) was composed of [ammonia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ammonia), [methane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methane), and hydrogen. One of his Chicago graduate students, [Stanley L. Miller](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanley_L._Miller), showed in the _[Miller–Urey experiment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miller%E2%80%93Urey_experiment)_ that, if such a mixture be exposed to electric sparks and to water, it can interact to produce [amino acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amino_acids), commonly considered the building blocks of life.

Urey and Failla taste testing of 1935 was inaccurate because neither one did not know some key data = Dunning Kruger effect.

Human gustatory ability is capable of tasting the difference between regular water And 'heavy' water.

In the last two decades or so, advancements in our understanding of human taste receptors have prompted a reopening of old cases like this – and in their new research, Ben Abu, Mason, and their team can finally confirm that there really is something a bit different about the taste of heavy water.

Physical chemist Pavel Jungwirth research has shown despite the fact that the two isotopes are nominally chemically identical, it is now conclusive scientifically that humans can distinguish by taste (which is based on chemical sensing) between H2O and D2O, with the latter having a distinct sweet taste. Urey and Failla's unequivocal opinion suffered from the Dunning Kruger effect on this topic and effectively stifled further research in this area for much of the next century, at least in terms of human taste-testing.

Other taste tests conducted by the team suggest why this is so, indicating that human taste receptivity to D2O is mediated by the taste receptor [TAS1R2/TAS1R3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TAS1R2), which is known to respond to sweetness in both natural sugars and artificial sweeteners. This is why processes foods and keto snacks that use deuterium often have an abnormal taste to some people.
Experiments in the lab with [HEK 293 cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HEK_293_cells) confirmed the same thing, showing robust responses in TAS1R2/TAS1R3 expressing cells when exposed to D2O.

In addition, computational modeling with molecular dynamics simulations revealed slight differences in the interactions between proteins and H2O versus D2O, which the team says needs further study to fully explain, but accords with [previous research](https://www.cell.com/biophysj/fulltext/S0006-3495\(02\)75666-X), and provides another example of [nuclear quantum effects](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41570-017-0109) are active in biochemical systems, including that [of water](https://www.pnas.org/content/116/7/2458). Since all life is water-based and mitochondria create water from reversing photosynthesis this has huge implications for modern medicine. Sadly this research will be buried by Big Pharma and centralized employed physicians will be used as an army to make sure this happens.

Decentralize clinicians have to keep pushing the edge of centralized science to get people to realize the fringe of medicine is where understanding life begins.

At a molecular level, this general behavior may be traced back to the slightly stronger hydrogen bonding in D2O vs H2O, called the Kinetic Isotope effect, which is due to a nuclear quantum effect, namely difference in [zero-point energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero-point_energy).
**Zero-point energy** (**ZPE**) is the lowest possible thermodynamic [energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy) state that a [quantum mechanical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_mechanical) system may have.
Unlike in [classical mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_mechanics), quantum systems constantly [fluctuate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_fluctuation) in their lowest energy state as described by the [Heisenberg uncertainty principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heisenberg_uncertainty_principle). Physics currently lacks a full theoretical model for understanding zero-point energy but this result in the sweetness of D2O shows us that human sensory receptors can detect changes in zero-point energy when it comes to the KIE of deuterium. What does this zero-point energy idea point to? The story of deuterium is a story of fermionic fields in quantum mechanics. In [quantum field theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field_theory), a **fermionic field** is a [quantum field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field) whose [quanta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum) are [fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermion).
The most prominent example of a fermionic field is the Dirac field, which describes fermions with [spin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin_\(physics\))\-1/2: [electrons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron), [protons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton), [quarks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quarks), **and neutrinos. This is why my April 2016 webinar is important. It elucidates the Dunning Kruger effect of modern science and medicine.
**
A popular proposal that attempts to address this issue is to say that the [fermion field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermion_field) has negative zero-point energy, while the [boson field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson_field) has positive zero-point energy, and thus these energies somehow cancel each other out. This idea would be true if [supersymmetry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supersymmetry) were an exact [symmetry of nature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_\(physics\)); however, the [LHC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LHC) at [CERN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CERN) has so far found no evidence to support it. Moreover, it is known that if supersymmetry is valid at all, it is at most a [broken symmetry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_breaking), only true at very high energies, and no one has been able to show a theory where zero-point cancellations occur in the low energy universe we observe today. As I said in the April 2016 webinar I believe inside the mitochondrial matrix I believe the answer lies using the low end of energy physics. We have no technological way yet to decipher these signals on the low end of the energy scale at the nanoscopic level that a mitochondrial matrix exists at in reality.

This discrepancy in science is known as the [cosmological constant problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmological_constant_problem) and it is one of the greatest [unsolved mysteries in physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unsolved_problems_in_physics). Many physicists believe that "the vacuum holds the key to a full understanding of nature, I believe the mitochondrial matrix holds this key and this new paper on Deuterium water being sweeter is proof that I am on the right track in my critical thinking.

Where is the other clue about zero-point energy in physics? It is in the physics of **l**[iquid helium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_helium). L[iquid helium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_helium) does not freeze under atmospheric pressure regardless of temperature due to its zero-point energy. Liquid helium retains [kinetic energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinetic_energy) and can not freeze regardless of temperature due to zero-point energy. When cooled below its [Lambda point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_point), it exhibits properties of [superfluidity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superfluidity). This is well known to science but superfluidity is still not well understood by physics.

The current edge of science says the uncertainty principle requires every quantum mechanical system to have fluctuating zero-point energy greater than the minimum of its classical [potential well](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potential_well).
This results in motion even at [absolute zero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_zero) temperature.

WHERE THIS PROBLEM LEADS:

All matter is, is light slowed down just enough to appear frozen so our senses can cipher its light energy to create a best guess. This is what E = mc^2 is all about. Nothing frozen is ever motionless in the quantum world.

Given the equivalence of mass and energy expressed by [Einstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein)'s [_E_ = _mc_2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/E_%3D_mc2), any point in [space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spacetime) that contains energy can be thought of as having mass to create particles. [Virtual particles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtual_particles) spontaneously flash into existence at every point in space due to the energy of [quantum fluctuations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_fluctuations) caused by the uncertainty principle. I think this is a hge deal in a mitochondrion. Modern physics has developed [quantum field theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field_theory) (QFT) to understand the fundamental interactions between matter and forces, it treats every single point of space as a [quantum harmonic oscillator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_oscillator_\(quantum\)). According to QFT the universe is made up of matter fields, whose [quanta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum) are [fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermions)(i.e. [leptons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lepton) and [quarks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quark)), and force fields, whose quanta are [bosons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson) (e.g. [photons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photon) and [gluons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gluon)). All these fields have zero-point energy. Recent experiments advocate the idea that particles themselves can be thought of as excited states of the underlying [quantum vacuum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_vacuum), and that all properties of matter are merely vacuum fluctuations arising from interactions of the zero-point field.

**The idea that "empty" space can have intrinsic energy associated with it, and that there is no such thing as a "true vacuum" is seemingly unintuitive.** I have found in my studies and research when you open your mind to the impossible you often find the truth that Nature hides.

It is often argued that the entire universe is completely bathed in zero-point radiation, and as such, it can add only some constant amount to calculations. Physical measurements will therefore reveal only deviations from this value. For many practical calculations, zero-point energy is dismissed by fiat in the mathematical model as a term that has no physical effect.

Such treatment causes problems, however, as in Einstein's theory of [general relativity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_relativity) the absolute energy value of space is not an arbitrary constant and gives rise to the [cosmological constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmological_constant). For decades most physicists assumed that there was some undiscovered fundamental principle that will remove the infinite zero-point energy and make it completely vanish. If the vacuum has no intrinsic, absolute value of energy it will not gravitate. It was believed that as the universe expands from the aftermath of the [Big Bang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Bang), the energy contained in any unit of empty space will decrease as the total energy spreads out to fill the volume of the universe; [galaxies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galaxies) and all matter in the universe should begin to decelerate. This possibility was ruled out in 1998 by the discovery that the expansion of the universe is not slowing down but is in fact accelerating, meaning empty space does indeed have some intrinsic energy. The discovery of [dark energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_energy) is best explained by zero-point energy, though it still remains a mystery as to why the value appears to be so small compared to the huge value obtained through theory - the [cosmological constant problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmological_constant_problem).

In any problem, always search for the crack, the anomaly, that crack is how the light enters and where the truth that nature uses, lies. Deuterium and liquid helium are two such anomalies.

We care.
Does the pandemic fearful public care if others die?
The price of human life seem to be a personal concern rather than any real compassion for others; So long as these (fearful) individuals are not the guinie pigs... the price others have to pay is OK with them. _These conversations I've had with these (fearful) individuals has been revealing for me._
